data.title: Treasury's Michael Faulkender steps down after five months - report
data.description:
Deputy Treasury Secretary Michael Faulkender is stepping down less than five months after his Senate confirmation, according to a media report published Friday.

This marks the second top Treasury departure this month, after U.S. President Donald Trump is ousted Internal Revenue Service Commisioner Billy Long just two months into the job.

Faulkender worked in the Treasury Department during Trump's first term. In the current term, Faulkender headed Treasury's transition team and helped shape the tax policies in Trump's tax and spending package signed last month.

Treasury did not immediately respond to Seeking Alpha's request for comment.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Housing market still stretched even with potential rate cuts – BofA analysts
data.description:
Housing affordability remains stretched even with potential rate cuts, requiring better wage growth or increased supply for meaningful improvement, according to Rafe Jadrosich, Senior U.S. Homebuilders and Building Products analyst.

The prospect of rate cuts has boosted homebuilding stocks (NYSEARCA:XHB), which have risen 16% since the end of June, outperforming the broader market in a pattern consistent with previous rate-cutting cycles.

“This recent rally reminds us a lot of what you saw in July and August of 2024 ahead of the September rate cut,” said Jadrosich, during a podcast conducted by BofA Global Research.

However, Jadrosich cautioned that “builders are trading now at 1.5-1.6x price-to-book – a company's stock price vs. its book value, which evaluates its market valuation relative to its assets – which is a little bit elevated compared to historical multiples heading into the first Fed rate cut.”

Also, despite 30-year fixed mortgage rates declining from 6.9% to 6.6% earlier this year, Jadrosich indicated in the interview that this 30-basis-point move has not significantly impacted demand.

“The new home market will certainly benefit if you have lower rates, especially if that’s in a backdrop where the macro environment is stable, it does increase demand, but affordability is still really stretched at current levels,” he said.

Jadrosich emphasized that “affordability probably only gets solved with better wage growth or more supply that comes onto the market.”

Housing markets are showing pronounced regional differences, with the Northeast, Mid-Atlantic, and Midwest outperforming while Florida and Texas experience weakness.

“Within Florida, it’s the entry level markets that are the softest, Jacksonville would stand out, and then some of the markets in southwest Florida that have been impacted by the cost of insurance,” Jadrosich explained.

He added that in Texas, “some of the Covid winners, cities like Austin and San Antonio that saw a lot of home price appreciation and in-migration, that’s where you’re seeing price cuts today.”

On a positive note, there appears to be encouraging progress in federal housing policy, with the Senate Banking Committee recently passing the ROAD to Housing Act of 2025.

According to Jadrosich, this bipartisan bill is “designed to spur construction and more housing through a mix of regulatory changes, studies, pilots, grant programs.”

While he cautioned that housing remains primarily a local issue, Jadrosich noted that the bill could “empower the Federal government through HUD to play a more useful role in tackling the housing shortage.”

Mark Cabana, head of U.S. Rates Strategy at BofA, also participated in the interview. He questioned if – with some signs of a moderating labor market – monetary policy is truly restrictive given the 4.2% unemployment rate and “very easy financial conditions, equities (SP500), (COMP:IND), (DJI) near all-time highs, credit spreads very tight.”

With the market pricing in a 75.5% probability of an interest rate cut of about 25 basis points at the September FOMC meeting, he believes a dovish Fed could push long rates lower despite inflation concerns.

data.title: UK regulator begins probe into Shutterstock/Getty Images deal
data.description:
The UK's antitrust regulator started an investigation into Getty Images's (NYSE:GETY) planned acquisition of Shutterstock (NYSE:SSTK). 

The UK's Competition and Markets Authority has a deadline of Oct. 20 to make a decision on the transaction, according to a filing on the regulator's website.

Getty Images (NYSE:GETY) agreed to purchase Shutterstock (SSTK) in a cash and stock deal in January, creating a new company valued at about $3.7 billion, including debt.

Shares of Getty Images (GETY) rose 5.8% on Friday, while Shutterstock gained 3%.

data.title: Tempus AI will acquire Paige for $81.25M
data.description:
• Tempus AI (NASDAQ:TEM) said that it will acquire Paige, an AI company focused on digital pathology, for $81.25M.
  • The deal amount is being paid predominantly in Tempus stock, as well as its assumption of Paige’s commitment under its existing Microsoft Azure cloud services deal.
  • The stock is up about 2% so far today.

data.title: U.S. stablecoin law spurs EU to accelerate digital euro plans - report
data.description:
Policymakers in the European Union are fast-tracking work on a digital euro after the U.S.'s landmark stablecoin law deepened concerns that dollar-backed tokens could crowd out Europe's currency in digital markets, according to a recent media report.

Last month, U.S. Congress passed the GENIUS Act, which set a new regulatory framework for the stablecoin sector and its issuers. A stablecoin is a digital token designed to keep a steady value to assets like the dollar.

After U.S. President Donald Trump signed the stablecoin legislation into law, EU policymakers started revisiting their digital euro plans, the Financial Times reported.

EU officials are weighing a shift from the once-expected private network to a public blockchain like Ethereum (ETH-USD) or Solana (SOL-USD), people familiar with the matter told the FT.

It's been years in which the European Central Bank has studied a digital euro. The monetary authority was said in May it was teaming up with nearly EU market participants to test the digital euro's payment functionalities and use cases through a newly launched innovation hub.

Central bank digital currencies, like the digital euro, are digital versions of cash issued and guaranteed by a country's central bank.

data.title: Honeywell names FARO Technologies CEO Lau to lead industrial automation unit
data.description:
Honeywell (NASDAQ:HON) said Friday it appointed former FARO Technologies head Peter Lau as the new President and CEO of its Industrial Automation business, effective October 15.

Lau, a former President of Honeywell's (NASDAQ:HON) Security, Fire and Electrical Products businesses, is returning to the company to succeed Lucian Boldea, who is leaving at the end of the month to pursue another opportunity.

Lau most recently served as President and CEO of 3D measuring systems company FARO Technologies, which was acquired in May by industrial tools maker Ametek, and previously served in leadership positions at Hubbell, GE and Catalyst Nutraceuticals.

Honeywell recently said it would evaluate strategic alternatives for parts of its Industrial Automation business ahead of the company's planned separation into three independent companies, which is expected to be complete in H2 2026.

data.title: Google remains clear search leader as competitors such as OpenAI still rely on it: report
data.description:
Despite the rise of artificial intelligence chatbots, such as OpenAI's ChatGPT helping users search for information, Google's (NASDAQ:GOOG)(NASDAQ:GOOGL) virtually infinite pool of search data continues to play a crucial role in these platforms, according to The Information.

OpenAI uses scraped Google search results to help fuel ChatGPT responses for current events such as news, sports scores and equity markets, according to the report, which cited people familiar with the issue. OpenAI utilizes a web-scraping firm known as SerpApi to harvest the data. Perplexity, another AI-powered search engine, is also a customer of SerpApi.

SerpApi also counts tech giants such as Nvidia (NVDA) and Meta Platforms (META) among its customers. 

SerpApi, which stands for Search Engines Results Page API, allows developers to collect data from Google and other search engines. According to SerpAPI, its webscraping services are legal in the U.S. as long as they collect from engines providing publicly available data. 

"We are committed to maintaining legal and ethical web scraping practices and providing our customers with peace of mind," according to the SerpAPI website. "This is one reason that for all plans from the production plan and upwards we offer our Legal US Shield. Although web scraping is legal in the US, we provide this coverage in case there were ever any issues to arise."

"We retrieve accurate, contextually relevant information from web pages and a variety of providers," OpenAI said to The Information. 

"I don't use Google anymore," said OpenAI CEO Sam Altman in an interview last week with The Verge. "I legitimately cannot tell you the last time I did a Google search."

Seeking Alpha reached out to OpenAI for comment.

data.title: Nasdaq, S&P, Dow rise on Powell's speech at Jackson Hole
data.description:
The stock market was upbeat on Friday as investors digest Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell's speech at Jackson Hole. 

S&P 500 (SP500) +1.3%, the Dow (DJI) +1.5%, and the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) +1.3%.

The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) fell 2 basis points to 4.31%. The 2-year yield (US2Y) was flat at 3.79%.

Wall Street closed lower on Thursday, pressured by declining shares of Walmart (WMT).

“The theme of 'good news is bad news' returned to markets yesterday following a strong US PMI release. This led investors to dial back expectations of Fed rate cuts…that leaves investors in a jittery mood going into the Jackson Hole Symposium,” Deutsche Bank's Peter Sidorov said. 

The main event for the day will be Powell's speech. He will speak on “Economic Outlook and Framework Review” before the 2025 Jackson Hole Economic Policy Symposium. Traders will hope to get any clues about the monetary policy outlook. Markets are expecting a rate cut in September. 

“Investors will be keenly watching whether Powell places more emphasis on weaker payrolls versus more stable measures of labor market slack and still solid activity and inflation data,” Sidorov added. 

Powell's speech is slated for 10 am ET. 

“Powell has lacked confidence and failed to articulate a medium-term vision for the economy at a time of uncertainty caused by structural change. The resulting mantra of 'data dependency' has not been helpful, as data has very obviously become significantly less reliable,” UBS' Paul Donovan said.

Today’s speech by Powell in theory looks at the economic outlook. It is to be hoped it will offer a robust defense of central bank independence too, Donovan added.

data.title: Biggest stock movers Friday: ZM, NIO, BJ, and more
data.description:
Stock futures ticked higher on Friday as investors awaited Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell’s speech at Jackson Hole, looking for clues on the outlook for monetary policy.

Here are some of Friday's biggest stock movers:

Biggest stock gainers

  • Zoom Communications (NASDAQ:ZM) +9% - Shares rose after delivering strong Q2 results and raising its full-year outlook. The company guided Q3 revenue to $1.21B–$1.22B (midpoint $1.215B vs. $1.21B est.) and EPS to $1.42–$1.44 (vs. $1.39 est.), while FY revenue was lifted to $4.83B–$4.84B (vs. prior $4.8B–$4.81B, $4.81B est.) and EPS to $5.81–$5.84 (vs. prior $5.56–$5.59, $5.60 est.). CEO Eric Yuan credited AI-driven innovation and efficiency gains, highlighting Zoom’s highest revenue growth in 11 quarters, a 9-pt GAAP operating margin expansion, and stronger free cash flow guidance of $1.74B–$1.78B.
  • NIO (NYSE:NIO) +8% - Shares surged after the company unveiled one of its most affordable vehicles yet, highlighting the intensifying price war in the domestic market. The new ES8 is offered in six- and seven-seat Executive Premium and Executive Signature Editions with starting prices of RMB 416,800 (RMB 308,800 with BaaS) and RMB 456,800 (RMB 348,800 with BaaS). The base ES8 is priced below the Tesla (TSLA) Model Y L six-seat SUV, and in Europe the model will be marketed as the EL8 due to a trademark dispute with Audi.
  • Biohaven (NYSE:BHVN) +6% - Shares jumped after the FDA canceled a planned advisory committee meeting tied to the review of its lead drug troriluzole for spinocerebellar ataxia, a rare neurodegenerative disorder. The biotech had received priority review for the NDA earlier this year, with the review timeline extended in May as regulators evaluated additional submissions. In an SEC filing, Biohaven disclosed that on Aug. 21 the FDA informed the company that an AdCom is no longer required, a move investors interpreted as a positive signal for approval prospects.
  •  

Biggest stock losers

  • Intuit (NASDAQ:INTU) -8% - Shares slipped after issuing slower growth guidance for Q1 and FY2026, overshadowing better-than-expected Q4 results. The QuickBooks and Credit Karma parent sees FY2026 revenue of $21.0B-$21.2B, up 12%-13% from last year, roughly in line with consensus, and projects non-GAAP EPS of $22.98-$23.18 vs. $22.99 expected. By segment, Global Business Solutions revenue is forecast to rise 14%-15% from 16% in FY2025, Consumer Group up 8%-9% vs. 10%, and Credit Karma up 10%-13% vs. 32%. For fiscal Q1, Intuit guided EPS of $3.05-$3.12, bracketing the $3.07 consensus, on revenue growth of 14%-15% compared with 20% in Q4.
  • BJ’s Wholesale Club (BJ) -8% - Shares fell after posting mixed Q2 results, with total comparable club sales down 0.3% due to lower fuel prices, though ex-gas comps rose 2.3%. Membership fee income climbed 9% on higher acquisition, retention, and premium penetration. Operating income rose 6.3%, and adjusted EBITDA gained 8%. Looking ahead, BJ’s guided FY comparable sales ex-gas to increase 2%–3.5% and raised adjusted EPS outlook to $4.20–$4.35 vs. the prior view of $4.10–$4.30 and the consensus of $4.30.
  • Workday (NASDAQ:WDAY) -6% - Shares dropped after reporting fiscal Q2 results and guidance that exceeded expectations but failed to spark enthusiasm. For Q3, the company guided subscription revenue of $2.24B, in line with estimates, while FY2026 subscription revenue is projected at $8.82B, slightly above Wall Street’s $8.8B forecast. Workday also raised its adjusted operating margin outlook to 29% from 28%, topping both its prior view and the 28.5% consensus. Alongside results, the company announced an agreement to acquire Paradox, an AI-powered candidate experience platform.

data.title: Stellantis bringing back the Jeep Cherokee
data.description:
Stellantis (NYSE:STLA) is reviving the Jeep Cherokee with added features and a turbo-four hybrid powertrain, aiming to reignite sales and strengthen its position in the U.S. SUV market.

“This is the biggest segment in the United States, and bringing this back into our portfolio is a perfect fit between the Compass and the Grand Cherokee,” Jeep CEO Bob Broderdorf said in an interview Friday morning on CNBC, adding that the decision to discontinue the Cherokee in 2023 was “obviously one of the largest misses from our sales decline.”

Former CEO Carlos Tavares shelved the Jeep Cherokee in 2023 as part of a cost-cutting initiative and to free up resources for EV production. Prior to the company’s decision to idle Cherokee production, sales of the vehicle were down ~61% for the first half of 2022.

But Stellantis (NYSE:STLA) is hoping the new Jeep will rekindle demand for the once iconic SUV with a new 1.6-liter turbo-four hybrid powertrain, 500 mile range on a single tank of gas, and a “technology-filled” interior that includes Stellantis’ new Connect One service package. The new model also has more interior and cargo space.

With a starting sticker price of $35,000, Stellantis (NYSE:STLA) has priced the Jeep Cherokee to compete with popular SUVs like the Honda (HMC) CR-V EX, Buick (GM) SUV lineup, and Toyota (TM) RAV4. However, the vehicle will be made at Stellantis' (STLA) Toluca, Mexico facility as a result of the closure of its prior home in Belvedere, Illinois, making the car liable for import taxes into the U.S.
